morphology.summary: A historically transparent compound-like form built around image and breaking.

etymology.summary: The word is traced through French and Medieval Latin to Byzantine Greek elements meaning image breaker.

semantic_drift.summary: The sense extends from religious image-breaking to figurative attacks on revered beliefs or institutions.

developments.description: The word is anchored in conflicts over sacred images.
Image-breaking becomes a metaphor for attacking symbolic authority.

present_usage.summary: Modern use ranges from literal image-destroyer to admired or criticized challenger of orthodoxy.

senses.definition: A person involved in destroying or rejecting religious images.

examples.text: The historian described the monk as an iconoclast in the Byzantine controversy.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

senses.definition: A person who attacks established beliefs, institutions, or conventions.

examples.text: The critic was celebrated as an iconoclast when the old canon began to shift.
examples.note: Original EI usage example for this capsule.

cultural_frame.summary: The word invokes religious conflict, orthodoxy, rebellion, and modern disruption.

frames.label: orthodoxy challenge
frames.description: Frames a person as confronting a protected symbolic order.

pragmatics.summary: The word can praise reforming courage or condemn destructive irreverence.

stances.label: praise
stances.description: Presents the person as brave or intellectually independent.

stances.label: criticism
stances.description: Presents the person as destructive toward valued inheritance.

expansion.paragraph: Iconoclast does not simply mean rebel. Its etymological frame is image-breaking, especially in religious controversy, and its modern figurative use applies that frame to attacks on revered beliefs or institutions. The pragmatic force depends on stance: it may praise courageous reform or warn about destructive irreverence.

trace.contribution: The word is traced through French and Medieval Latin to Byzantine Greek elements meaning image breaker.
The sense extends from religious image-breaking to figurative attacks on revered beliefs or institutions.
The word can praise reforming courage or condemn destructive irreverence.

capsule_summary: A stance-sensitive word for a breaker of icons, literal or figurative, whose meaning ranges from religious image-destroyer to reforming challenger.

provenance.note: Used as a cited lexical source; capsule wording is manually normalized and layered.

unresolved_questions: Add non-Wiktionary scholarly citations for Byzantine and Reformation historical usage.
